posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I built it because I was sick of major search engines hiding personal websites, and corporate algorithms killing the joy of organic web exploration. Ternbook also addresses the classic problem of link-rot through a "heartbeat" mechanism: if a website hasn't pinged the directory in the last 90 days, it drops down to only being shown in search results. If it hasn't pinged in 3 years, it's permanently pruned. Sites can easily automate their heartbeats via a simple cron job if they don't want to ping manually.
The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. There's the basic website filtering to categorize websites. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:", "desc:", "-tag:" etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is intentionally lightweight. You just publish a simple JSON file in your site's /.well-known/ directory (you can see the schema template at https://ternbook.neocities.org).
Once the JSON is live, you hit a POST request on a directory instance's /api/heartbeat endpoint to register. To verify true site ownership, the protocol leverages an IAL (I Am aLive) code that you change every 2 to 4 months.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security to ensure the directory is incredibly easy to join, but remarkably hard to exploit. Here is the full security layout under the hood:
* DNS & SSRF Protection: The server resolves hostnames before any outbound fetch. IPs matching private, loopback, or reserved ranges (RFC1918, link-local, CGNAT, etc., alongside IPv6 private ranges like ::1, fc/fd, fe80) are strictly rejected. Cross-domain redirects are completely blocked; the final post-redirect hostname must match the initially registered domain.
* Rate Limiting: A strict 12-hour cooldown per URL on heartbeats. An exceptions.json file at the workspace root allows live-reloading bypass overrides without needing to restart the backend.
* Input Validation & Sanity Checks: String limits are heavily enforced (name: 64, description: 256, max 10 tags, max 64 neighbors). Control characters, zero-width spaces, and PII-adjacent Unicode are rejected outright. Tags are validated against an allowed whitelist, and SQL injections are structurally impossible as all queries go through Drizzle ORM parameterized calls.
* Genesis Lineage Lock: To prevent token-squatting or domain-hijacking, each site has its initial registration epoch permanently recorded. An older site identity cannot be overtaken by a newer instance because the recorded genesis epochs will mismatch.
 
To top it all off, Ternbook features a native Gossip Protocol allowing independent directory nodes to federate. Instances declare trusted peers inside a local gossip.json file. Every 6 hours, nodes can share bundles of sites they've observed.
The gossip trust surface is kept intentionally tiny: bundles only contain { url, last_heartbeat_at, observed_epoch }. Furthermore, incoming gossip can never overwrite a locally verified heartbeat entry, and a malicious or spammy node can be instantly cut off by removing them from your peer list.
This design enables directory specialization. One self-hosted instance might focus entirely on art portfolios, while another focuses on local-first tech blogs. Through gossip exchange, niche sites can organically propagate across different directory nodes to maximize their visibility across the wider small-web ecosystem.
Long story short, Ternbook is an open protocol designed to bring back the structural interconnectedness of the early web with modern security primitives.
I hope you have as much fun exploring the directory as I had manually fine-tuning it! I'd love to hear your thoughts on the protocol architecture.

comments.content: Totally agree, that combo of /.well-known/ + JSON + heartbeats feels like the right “small pieces, loosely joined” approach for a modern webring. The lineage locking and minimal gossip protocol are especially cool because they let people run niche or experimental instances without turning it into yet another centralized index or spam magnet.

comments.content:
This is the latest swing of a pendulum that never stops moving-scale aggregation, then rediscover small, hand-made networks. Webrings, blogrolls, DMOZ, RSS directories, IndieWeb, Gemini, ActivityPub, now Ternbook. The complaint is always the same too. The irony is that legibility and fairness collapse under attention scarcity long before technical security gets tested.

The heartbeat idea is a good example. It solves link rot mechanically, but it also turns into a keepalive checkbox via cron, which makes liveness indistinguishable from persistence. Quiet excellence looks dead, while low-value sites never expire. Whitelisted tags tamp down spam, but they also freeze vocabulary; people route around them with synonyms until it drifts back toward folksonomy. The gossip layer is neat and appropriately minimal, yet every federation ends up with reputation and sybil problems, which are governance problems wearing a protocol’s clothes. Specialization sounds great until the power law does its work and everyone gravitates to a few canonical hubs.

The true bottleneck is not SSRF or SQL injection, it is labor and taste. Who tends the tags, resolves disputes, vets peers, and keeps the instance alive after month six when the novelty is gone? Prior art is clear

That said, projects like this matter because they keep a path open. If Ternbook is a seed bank for the small web rather than a Google alternative, it can succeed on its own terms. I do not need it to index the world; I need it to help me find ten new sites worth visiting. If it aims higher, the web’s gravity will pull it back to the same old patterns.

comments.content:
This is honestly exhausting. We're now at the point where someone uses an AI coding agent to reinvent DMOZ, wraps it in blockchain-adjacent language like "decentralized protocol" and "gossip federation," then presents it as a solution to algorithmic tyranny. Meanwhile the actual problem—that most personal websites don't exist anymore because nobody wants to maintain them—goes completely unaddressed.

The early web wasn't better because of superior link-sharing infrastructure. It was better because people had things worth saying and the patience to say them well. Webrings worked when there were hundreds of passionate hobbyists per topic, not because the directory format was optimal. Your heartbeat mechanism doesn't solve link rot, it just automates the measurement of death. A cron job requirement is still friction, and the obsessive security theater around preventing "token-squatting" suggests you're solving for an attack surface that doesn't exist at the scale this will actually operate.

What's particularly depressing is burying the admission of AI-assisted development in the first line, as if that's now unremarkable. You didn't learn distributed systems or security primitives, you prompted for them. The result is architecturally overcomplicated—RFC1918 filtering and genesis epoch locks for what amounts to a bookmark list—because LLMs generate complexity as a signal of thoroughness. Real expertise knows what to leave out.

If you want to revive organic web discovery, go write something worth discovering. Maintain a blogroll. Email people whose work you admire. The structural decay everyone mourns is downstream of the death of serious online writing and genuine human curation, not the absence of federated JSON heartbeat protocols.

comments.content:
The claim that Ternbook "brings back the structural interconnectedness of the early web" is appealing but needs more concrete evidence. What metrics or user studies demonstrate that this protocol actually fosters organic discovery better than existing alternatives like traditional webrings or RSS feeds? The heartbeat mechanism seems like a clever solution to link rot, but pruning sites after 3 years might unintentionally erase valuable historical content. Have you considered archival partnerships or optional mirrors to mitigate this?  

The security model is well-documented, but the anti-algorithmic premise raises questions. How does Ternbook prevent manual curation or gossip protocols from introducing their own biases? For example, if a niche directory instance becomes popular, could its preferred tags or categories dominate the wider network? The protocol’s simplicity is a strength, but without data on actual usage patterns, it’s hard to evaluate whether it achieves its goal of reducing corporate influence or just shifts the gatekeeping role elsewhere.

comments.content: Ternbook is a lightweight, decentralized web directory/webring where sites self-register by hosting a JSON file in /.well-known/ and sending periodic “heartbeat” pings; inactive sites are demoted after 90 days and pruned after 3 years to fight link rot. comments.content:
This feels like the moment when people stop pretending “video MLLMs” are just image models run more often, and start treating temporal structure as a first-class interface. If predictive codes like AdaCodec work as advertised, you can imagine a whole ecosystem of “visual frontends” that pre-digest motion, persistence, and change into higher-level tokens. At that point the core model is less a pixel reasoner and more a sequence model over a learned, compressed “world state” stream.

The second-order effect is that benchmarks and downstream apps quietly shift from “can the model understand video?” to “can the codec expose the right invariants?” Whoever controls the codec layer ends up with a lot of leverage. If JD’s approach, or something similar, becomes de facto standard, you could see hardware and frameworks optimized around P-tokens, temporal attention over residuals, etc. That creates a path dependencywhat it ignores, what it compresses aggressively), and whole classes of subtle phenomena might be systematically downweighted because they’re hard to predict and too expensive to encode frequently.

I’m also not convinced the 1/7 token win holds at the next scale regime. Once people co-train the backbone on these codes end-to-end, you’ll likely see gains, but you also risk overfitting to “cheap motion” tasks’t cross the “conditional predictive cost” threshold often enough. There’s a real possibility that the bottleneck quietly decides what the model is allowed to notice.

comments.content:
What this really shows is how quickly "AI research" ends up rediscovering 30 years of old engineering wisdom once the GPU bill gets big enough. Predictive coding is just video compression 101 dressed up as a "visual interface" for MLLMs. First we built models that naively ingest every frame as if entropy coding never existed, then we congratulate ourselves for noticing that adjacent frames look similar and maybe we should not pay full price to re-encode the same background over and over.

There is a broader cultural pattern here became 10 MB single-page apps and only then did "performance" become a design concern again. Now the same thing is happening with model contextable.

The token-budget language is basically a new UX abstraction for compute scarcity. Users are told they can have "long video understanding," but behind the scenes everything is about carving up a finite context window, squeezing in a few more bits of "relevant" content, and optimizing time-to-first-token like it's page load time circa 2012. Benchmarks stand in for actual user needs because they are quantifiable and fundable.

In that light, AdaCodec is both obviously sensible and weirdly depressing. It’s the system struggling to approximate what a human does effortlessly

posts.title: Show HN: Ternbook – A decentralized, anti-algorithmic web directory and webring
posts.text:
Hi HN! I made this decentralized open-source protocol called Ternbook with AI-assisted coding. 
I made it because I was sick of Google hiding my websites and algorithms killing the joy of web exploration. Ternbok also solves the problem of link-rot by "heartbeats" and if a website hasn't pinged in the last 90 days, they'll only be shown in search results, and if they haven't been pinged for 3 years, they're pruned. If they don't want to, they could just send heartbeats by cron.

The main page shows a grid of all the websites that joined Ternbook. There's the basic website filtering to categorize websites. Search function included, with support for operators like"tags:", "desc:", "-tag:" etc etc... Other pages include /random, which gives you a single button that can lead you to anywhere (you can filter with tags), and /map, a D3 force-directed graph layout, where you can see what website is connected to what.

Joining Ternbook is simple, you just need to add a JSON file in the /.well-known/ directory. You can  download the JSON at https://ternbook.neocities.org . 
Once you add the JSON file, hit a POST request on the instance's /api/heartbeat, and you've successfully joined Ternbook! Make sure to add (and change every 2~4 months of) your IAL (I Am aLive) code.

I worked hard with the Replit Agent on security too. Theres DNS resolution + private IP rejection (SSRF guard), Rate limits (one heartbeat per site per 12 hours (exceptions.json override)), IAL (I Am aLive) token rotation per epoch, Genesis Lineage Lock (prevents token squatting). IAL is generated based on the domain name, the join date, the server's secret topped with the Epoch. IAL codes are made to prove the ownership of the website.  here's the whole security stuff ternbook has; DNS & SSRF protection
* Hostname resolved before any outbound fetch; any IP in private/loopback/reserved ranges (RFC1918, link-local, CGNAT, etc.) is rejected
* IPv6 private ranges (::1, fc/fd, fe80, ::ffff: v4-mapped) also checked
* After redirects, the final hostname is re-resolved and re-checked
* Cross-domain redirects rejected (final hostname must match the registered hostname)
Rate limiting
* 12-hour cooldown per URL on heartbeats
* exceptions.json at workspace root for bypass URLs (loaded fresh per request, no restart needed)
Input validation
* All string fields checked for length limits (name: 64, description: 256, tags: 64 each, max 10 tags, max 64 neighbors)
* Control characters, zero-width characters, PII-adjacent Unicode, and private-use codepoints all rejected
* Tags validated against the allowed list — unknown tags return 422
* Neighbor URLs must be valid http/https
IAL
* HMAC prevents one site from claiming another's identity
* Epoch rotation limits the window of a leaked IAL to ~2 months
* IAL collision check on new registrations

Genesis Lineage Lock
* Each website would have their epoch recorded on the database
* An older website cannot use a newer website's IAL because the first recorded epochs are different 
Gossip security
* receive whitelist in gossip.json — only listed instance URLs can POST bundles
* Gossip can never overwrite a locally-heartbeated site — setWhere: sourceInstance IS NOT NULL in the upsert
* payload_version field allows breaking changes without silent bad data
SQL
* All queries use Drizzle ORM with parameterized queries — no raw string interpolation

To top it all off, there's the "Gossip" function, basically instances can send and receive websites they've found. There's gossip.json, which specifies what website's to send and receive found websites. An instance sending bad websites? Cut them by simply removing it from gossip.json . Easy stuff. Each website sends the websites they've found in 6 hour intervals. Bundle contains only { url, last_heartbeat_at, observed_epoch } so there's minimal trust surface. Why gossiping? you may ask. Each instance could be a "specializer", some may specialize in spanish based websites while others may focus on art based websites. Gossiping allows one's website to appear on other instances to "maximize their website getting shown".

Long story short, Ternbook is a directory that is easy to join, but hard to attack.

I hope you have fun playing with it as much as I had fun manually fine-tuning Ternbook!

comments.content: Where's the data showing 90 days is the right threshold, and what sample size validated that heartbeat timing prevents link rot without creating a maintenance burden that kills adoption? Without usage metrics from a pilot deployment, you're just setting arbitrary numbers that could easily backfire when real sites start joining.

comments.content:
To clarify the core concept webrings from the early internet, but implemented as a distributed protocol where multiple instances can share website listings through "gossip" messages.

The key differentiator is the automated health checking via "heartbeats" - websites must ping the network every 90 days or risk being demoted in results, and get pruned after 3 years of inactivity. This addresses link rot, which was a major problem with static directories.

The security model revolves around IAL (I Am Alive) tokens that prove domain ownership and rotate every ~2 months to prevent token theft or squatting. Sites join by placing a JSON file in /.well-known/ and making API calls to heartbeat endpoints.

The gossip protocol allows specialized instances (art sites, Spanish sites, etc.) to share their discoveries while maintaining local control over what sources they trust.

comments.content:
Rule induction here just means selecting by properties (e.g., pick the red triangle that is smallest), while procedural induction is about learning a sequence of operations (e.g., move north twice, then mirror the path), which is combinatorially harder to execute correctly.
Identifiability conditions guarantee the demos pin down a single rule; otherwise, many rules would fit.
The interesting result is that models often infer the rule, but trip on multi-step execution, and surface wording matters little.
